conditionDescriptions: History of hyperkalemia with RAS inhibitor combination therapy
contraindications.clinicalRationale: Combination therapy with multiple RAS blocking agents was associated with higher rate of hyperkalemia and acute kidney injury compared to single RAS inhibitor use, with no additional kidney or cardiovascular benefit demonstrated in long-term trials
contraindications.alternativeConsiderations: Use only one agent at a time to block the RAS
contraindications.sourceSnippet: Long-term outcome trials in patients with diabetes and CKD demonstrated no kidney or cardiovascular benefit of RAS blockade with combined therapy to block the RAS versus the single use of RAS inhibitors. However, combination therapy was associated with a higher rate of hyperkalemia and AKI, and thus only one agent at a time should be used to block the RAS.

warnings.warningText: Monitor for acute eGFR decrease with RASi initiation
warnings.affectedPopulation: Patients with diabetes and CKD initiating RASi
warnings.actionRequired: Tolerate acute eGFR decrease of ≤30% with initiation of RASi, monitor kidney function

warnings.warningText: RASi should be titrated to maximum tolerated dose in patients with hypertension and diabetes with CKD
warnings.affectedPopulation: Patients with diabetes, CKD and hypertension
warnings.actionRequired: Titrate RAS inhibitor to maximum antihypertensive or maximum tolerated dose

warnings.warningText: Monitor serum potassium concentration when adding nonsteroidal MRA to RASi therapy
warnings.affectedPopulation: Patients with T2D and CKD receiving combination RASi and nonsteroidal MRA therapy
warnings.actionRequired: Ensure normal serum potassium concentration before and after adding nonsteroidal MRA to RASi, implement standardized potassium-monitoring protocol
